questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A positive point charge $Q$ is placed close to an isolated metal cube.
options: The interior remains free of charge and the surface develops a nonuniform charge distribution.
The cube acquires a net positive charge.
The interior becomes positively charged while the surface becomes negatively charged.
The cube acquires a net negative charge.
questions.solution:
When a positive point charge is placed near an isolated metal cube, electrostatic induction takes place.

The free electrons in the metal are attracted towards the positive charge, causing the surface closer to the charge to become negatively charged and the farther surface to become positively charged. This results in a nonuniform charge distribution on the surface.

Since the cube is isolated, its net charge remains zero.

In electrostatic equilibrium, the electric field inside a conductor is zero. According to Gauss's law, the net charge inside any closed surface entirely within the conductor must be zero. Thus, the interior of the cube remains completely free of charge.

questions.subject: Physics
options: Gauss's law is valid only for charges situated in a vacuum.
Gauss's law is applicable only to symmetrical charge distributions.
The flux of the electric field across a closed surface due to all charges is equal to the flux due to the charges enclosed by the surface.
The electric field computed by Gauss's law is the field produced by the charges inside the Gaussian surface.
questions.solution:
Gauss's law states that the total electric flux through any closed surface is equal to $\dfrac{1}{\epsilon_0}$ times the net charge enclosed by the surface.

The electric field $\vec{E}$ in the expression $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \dfrac{q_{enclosed}}{\epsilon_0}$ is the resultant electric field due to all charges present in space, both inside and outside the Gaussian surface.

However, the electric flux contributed by the charges situated outside the closed surface is zero, because any field line from an outside charge that enters the surface must also exit it. Therefore, the total flux of the electric field across the closed surface due to all charges is exactly equal to the flux due to only the charges enclosed by the surface.

Gauss's law is valid for any charge distribution (not just symmetrical ones) and in any medium (using the appropriate permittivity).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large nonconducting sheet $M$ carries a uniform charge density. Two small, uncharged metal rods $A$ and $B$ are positioned near the sheet, as shown in the figure.
options: The sheet $M$ attracts rod $A$.
Rod $B$ repels rod $A$.
The sheet $M$ attracts rod $B$.
Rod $A$ attracts rod $B$.
questions.solution:
Let the large nonconducting sheet $M$ have a positive charge density (the reasoning remains the same for a negative charge).

The electric field produced by a large (but finite) sheet decreases with distance.

This electric field polarizes the uncharged metal rods $A$ and $B$. For rod $A$, the end closer to $M$ acquires a negative charge, and the farther end acquires a positive charge.

Since the electric field of $M$ is stronger at the closer end, the attractive force on the negative charge is greater than the repulsive force on the positive charge. Thus, the net force is attractive, and $M$ attracts rod $A$.

Similarly, rod $B$ is polarized with a negative charge on its closer end and a positive charge on its farther end. Because the field of $M$ continues to decrease with distance, the attractive force on the closer end of $B$ is greater than the repulsive force on its farther end. Thus, $M$ also attracts rod $B$.

Furthermore, the right end of rod $A$ (positively charged) and the left end of rod $B$ (negatively charged) are adjacent to each other. Since they have opposite charges, they attract each other. Thus, rod $A$ attracts rod $B$.

Therefore, options (1), (3), and (4) are correct.

Answer: The sheet $M$ attracts rod $A$.; The sheet $M$ attracts rod $B$.; Rod $A$ attracts rod $B$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the flux of the electric field through a closed surface is zero,
options: the charge in the surroundings of the surface must be zero
the electric field may be zero at all points on the surface
the charge enclosed within the surface must be zero
the electric field must be zero at all points on the surface
questions.solution:
According to Gauss's law, the net electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = \dfrac{q_{enclosed}}{\epsilon_0}$.

Given that the flux $\Phi = 0$, we have $q_{enclosed} = 0$. This implies that the net charge enclosed within the surface must be zero.

The electric field at points on the surface is the resultant field due to both enclosed and external charges. If the net enclosed charge is zero and there are no external charges, the electric field will be zero at all points on the surface. However, if there are external charges, the electric field will not be zero, even though the net flux remains zero. Thus, the electric field may or may not be zero at all points on the surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed surface $S$ is set up around a conducting wire that is connected to a battery and a switch, as shown in the figure. When the switch is closed, the free electrons within the wire begin to travel along it. During any given time interval, the number of electrons entering the closed surface $S$ equals the number leaving it. Upon closing the switch, the electric field flux through the closed surface
options: remains unchanged
decreases
remains zero
increases
questions.solution:
According to Gauss's law, the electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{\text{enclosed}}}{\epsilon_0}$.

Initially, when the switch is open, the wire is electrically neutral, so the net charge enclosed by the surface $S$ is zero ($q_{\text{enclosed}} = 0$). Thus, the initial electric flux is zero.

When the switch is closed, current starts flowing. The problem explicitly states that the number of electrons entering the closed surface $S$ equals the number leaving it during any given time interval. This means there is no net accumulation or depletion of charge inside the surface $S$.

Therefore, the net charge enclosed by the surface $S$ remains zero ($q_{\text{enclosed}} = 0$) even after the switch is closed.

Consequently, the electric flux through the closed surface $S$ remains zero. Since the flux was zero initially and continues to be zero, it remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a charge $q$ is situated at the centre of a hemisphere. A second charge $Q$ is positioned at one of the locations $A$, $B$, $C$, and $D$. For which location(s) of this second charge does the flux of the electric field through the hemisphere remain unchanged?
questions.solution:
The initial electric flux through the curved surface of the hemisphere due to the charge $q$ at the centre is $\Phi_{q} = \dfrac{q}{2\epsilon_0}$.

When a second charge $Q$ is placed at any external point, the total flux through the hemisphere becomes $\Phi_{\text{total}} = \Phi_{q} + \Phi_{Q}$, where $\Phi_{Q}$ is the flux due to the charge $Q$.

For the total flux to remain unchanged, the flux through the curved surface due to $Q$ must be zero, i.e., $\Phi_{Q} = 0$.

Consider a closed surface formed by the curved hemispherical surface and its flat circular base. By Gauss's law, the net flux through this closed surface due to an external charge $Q$ is zero.

$\Phi_{\text{closed}} = \Phi_{\text{curved}} + \Phi_{\text{base}} = 0 \Rightarrow \Phi_{\text{curved}} = -\Phi_{\text{base}}$

For points $A$ and $C$, the charge $Q$ lies exactly on the plane containing the flat base of the hemisphere. The electric field lines produced by $Q$ at any point on the base will lie in the plane of the base.

Since the area vector of the base is perpendicular to its plane, the electric field $\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the area vector $d\vec{A}$ everywhere on the base.

Thus, the flux through the base is $\Phi_{\text{base}} = \int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{A} = 0$.

Consequently, $\Phi_{\text{curved}} = 0$ for charges placed at $A$ and $C$.

For points $B$ and $D$, the charge is not in the plane of the base, so field lines will cross the base, making $\Phi_{\text{base}} \neq 0$ and thus $\Phi_{\text{curved}} \neq 0$.

Therefore, the flux through the hemisphere remains unchanged only when the second charge is placed at locations $A$ and $C$.

Answer: $A$; $C$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A closed surface intersecting a conducting sphere is depicted in the figure. If a positive charge is positioned at point $P$, the flux of the electric field through the closed surface
options: will become negative
will remain zero
will become positive
will become undefined
questions.solution:
When a positive charge is placed at point P, it induces a negative charge on the closer side (right side) of the conducting sphere due to electrostatic attraction.

Consequently, an equal amount of positive charge is induced on the farther side (left side) of the conducting sphere due to repulsion.

As seen in the figure, the closed surface encloses the left portion of the conducting sphere. Therefore, the net charge enclosed by the closed surface is positive.

According to Gauss's law, the electric flux through a closed surface is given by $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{\text{enclosed}}}{\epsilon_0}$.

Since the enclosed charge $q_{\text{enclosed}}$ is positive, the electric flux through the closed surface will become positive.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric dipole is positioned at the centre of a sphere. Choose the correct statements:
options: The electric field is non-zero at all points on the sphere
The electric field is zero along a circle on the sphere
The electric field is zero at every point on the sphere
The electric flux through the sphere is zero
questions.solution:
The electric field due to a short dipole at a distance $r$ from its centre is given by $E = \dfrac{p}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^3} \sqrt{1 + 3\cos^2\theta}$, where $\theta$ is the angle made by the position vector with the dipole moment.

For any point on the sphere of radius $r$, the magnitude of the electric field is minimum at the equatorial plane ($\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$), where $E = \dfrac{p}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r^3}$, which is non-zero. Thus, the electric field is non-zero at all points on the sphere.

An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges, $+q$ and $-q$. The net charge enclosed by the sphere is $q_{enclosed} = +q - q = 0$.

According to Gauss's law, the net electric flux through the sphere is $\Phi = \dfrac{q_{enclosed}}{\epsilon_0} = 0$.
